votes.reason: The Python code correctly translates the precondition and the specification, checking if the three sides satisfy the Pythagorean theorem.
The Python code directly translates the Coq specifications, checking the same conditions for the pre-condition and the Pythagorean theorem for the post-condition.
The Python code correctly implements the preconditions and the specification, checking if the three sides satisfy the Pythagorean theorem in any order.

The Python code correctly implements the precondition (all sides positive) and the specification (res is True iff one of the three Pythagorean arrangements holds). The Coq spec uses a biconditional (res = true <-> condition), which means res should be true exactly when the condition holds and false otherwise. The Python code checks res == is_right, which is equivalent - res matches the boolean value of whether any Pythagorean arrangement is satisfied. Both will produce the same concrete results for all inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the precondition (all sides positive) and the specification (res is True iff one of the three Pythagorean arrangements holds). The Coq spec uses a biconditional (res = true <-> condition), which means res should be true exactly when the condition holds and false otherwise. The Python code checks res == is_right, which is equivalent: when is_right is True, res must be True for the spec to hold; when is_right is False, res must be False. Both implementations check the same three Pythagorean conditions.

The Python code correctly implements the precondition (all sides positive) and the specification (checking if res matches whether any permutation of the Pythagorean theorem holds). The Coq spec uses a biconditional (res = true <-> condition), which means res should be true iff the condition holds, and false otherwise. The Python code checks res == is_right, which is equivalent: when is_right is True, res must be True; when is_right is False, res must be False. This matches the Coq specification's concrete behavior.
Precondition matches positivity, and the spec checks res equals the Pythagorean condition under any permutation using unbounded integers, matching Coq Z behavior.

Both preconditions require all sides > 0, and the spec checks res is true exactly when any permutation satisfies the Pythagorean equality, using exact integer arithmetic. Python’s logic mirrors the Coq spec on all inputs.

Both preconditions require all sides > 0, and the spec checks that res is true iff any permutation satisfies the Pythagorean equality. Python integer arithmetic matches Coq Z arithmetic, so concrete outcomes align.
